title: Rewards and Penalties: Teaching Good Behavior
concept: Reward signals — points earned for good actions and penalties for bad ones — gradually shape an agent's behavior over many repeated trials, the same way treats shape a dog's behavior.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In reinforcement learning, what is the environment?

options.text: The world the agent acts within
The agent's decision-making program
A single choice the agent makes

exercises.explain: Right! The environment is everything surrounding and reacting to the agent.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An action is one specific choice available to the agent.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Actions are the individual moves an agent can choose from.

cards.title: Treats, Sit, Repeat
cards.body: A dog doesn't understand the word "sit" the first time you say it. But say the command, and the moment its rear hits the floor, hand over a treat. Do this dozens of times, and the dog starts sitting on command — not because it understood English, but because sitting reliably earned a treat.

cards.title: What a reward signal actually does
cards.body: A reward signal is a number handed to the agent after an action — positive for good outcomes, negative (a penalty) for bad ones. On its own, one reward barely teaches anything. It's the pattern across many trials that shapes behavior.

cards.title: Warmer, warmer, cold — a guessing game
cards.body: Think of the game where a friend says "warmer" as you get closer to a hidden object and "colder" as you move away. No single "warmer" tells you the exact spot, but after enough rounds, those small signals steer you right to it. Rewards and penalties work the same way over many attempts.

cards.title: Grading a video-game boss fight
cards.body: A game-playing agent takes a hit and loses health: penalty. It lands an attack: small reward. It defeats the boss: big reward. Across thousands of attempts, actions that led to more hits and fewer losses start happening more often.

cards.title: One reward doesn't teach the whole lesson
cards.body: You might assume a single reward instantly "fixes" an agent's behavior, like flipping a switch. It doesn't. Behavior shifts gradually, trial by trial, as patterns of reward and penalty build up across many attempts — just like a dog needs many repetitions, not one treat, to reliably sit on command.

right.text:
Good actions repeat more,
bad ones fade out

exercises.prompt: Why does a dog eventually sit on command after many rounds of treats?

options.text: Because sitting has repeatedly been followed by a reward
Because it understands the English word "sit"
Because one treat instantly teaches the full behavior

exercises.explain: Right! Repeated pairing of the action with a reward is what shapes the behavior.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single reward is usually enough to permanently teach an agent the best behavior.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Behavior shifts gradually across many trials of reward and penalty, not from one signal alone.

exercises.sentence: Reward and penalty signals shape an agent's behavior gradually, across many ___.
exercises.explain: It takes repeated attempts — trials — for reward patterns to actually change behavior.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best shows a reward signal shaping behavior over time?

options.text: A cat starts running to the kitchen at the sound of a can opener because that sound has repeatedly preceded food
A thermostat displays the current temperature on a screen
A spreadsheet sorts a column of numbers

exercises.explain: Right! The cat's behavior changed because that sound reliably paired with a reward (food) many times.

exercises.prompt: A robot vacuum bumps into a wall and its motor briefly reverses (a small penalty). After hundreds of runs, it bumps into far fewer walls. What's happening?

options.text: The repeated penalty for bumping walls is gradually discouraging that action
The single penalty from the first bump permanently fixed the behavior
Penalties have no effect on future actions
The vacuum was reprogrammed by a person after every bump

exercises.explain: Exactly! Across hundreds of trials, the repeated penalty makes wall-bumping actions less common.

exercises.prompt: A language-learning app gives a small reward (a streak point) for a correct answer and a small penalty (a lost heart) for a wrong one. After many days of practice, a student's accuracy on certain question types improves. Which explanation best fits how reward signals work?

options.text: The repeated pattern of rewards and penalties across many attempts gradually reinforced the actions (answers) that worked
A single streak point instantly taught the student every correct answer
Rewards and penalties only matter on the very first attempt
The student's improvement has nothing to do with the reward signals

exercises.explain: Exactly! It's the accumulation of reward and penalty signals across many trials that gradually shapes better behavior.

exercises.prompt: A theme park trains a dolphin to jump through a hoop by giving it a fish every time it jumps a little higher than before, and withholding the fish if it doesn't. Over several weeks, the dolphin reliably jumps through the hoop. Why does this take weeks rather than a single session?

options.text: Because behavior is shaped gradually — each rewarded attempt slightly reinforces the action, and it takes many repetitions to build reliable behavior
Because dolphins cannot learn from rewards at all
Because the fish is a penalty, not a reward
Because the hoop is the agent in this scenario

exercises.explain: Exactly! Just like the dog-and-treat example, reliable behavior builds up gradually from many rewarded trials, not from a single signal. Next: what happens when an agent has to choose between trying something new and sticking with what already works.
